q: A changing magnetic field could produce a current.
In a DC, the electrons flow steadily in the same direction all the time.
The current delivered by a battery is a direct current.
The current delivered by a generator is an alternating current.
The current that flows in a flashlight is a direct current.
The current that flows in a cell phone is a direct current.
The current that flows in a laptop is a direct current.
The current that flows in any appliance running on the batteries is direct current.
The direct current is of great industrial importance.
The current flowing in power lines is direct current.
The normal household electricity from a wall outlet is direct current.
The voltage used in Myanmar is between 220 V and 240 V.
The generators provide the electricity used in our modern society.
A galvanometer is used to measure very small current.
The alternating current induced in a rotating coil is constant with time.
A rotating coil in an electric field is the basis of an AC generator.
An AC generator is a device for generating direct current.
The alternating current is universally used for household electricity.
The alternating current is relatively hard to change the voltage of the current.
The inevitable loss of energy that occurs in long distant distribution is far smaller with alternating current than with direct current.
Joseph Henry and Michael Faraday together discovered the methods of producing electric current from a magnetic field.
As soon as a current flows through a wire loop, a momentary current flows in the loop near it.
As soon as a current stops flowing through a wire loop, a momentary current flows in the loop near it.
If a constant current is flowing through a stationary wire loop, no current is induced in the stationary loop near it.
If a current-carrying wire loop is moved abruptly towards or away from a second loop near it, an induced current flows in the second loop.
If a magnet is moved abruptly towards or away from a wire loop near it, an induced current flows in the wire loop.
An induced current will flow in a wire loop when a current in a wire loop near it is ON and OFF.
An induced current will flow in a wire loop, when a current-carrying wire loop is moved abruptly towards or away from it.
An induced current will flow in a wire loop, when a magnet is moved abruptly towards or away from it.
The change in magnetic flux through a wire loop produces an induced emf.
The magnitude of the induced emf in a wire loop is inversely proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through that loop.
An induced current will never be produced in a stationary wire loop.
An induced current will always be produced whenever there is relative motion between a wire loop and a magnetic field.
An induced current will always be produced whenever there is non-parallel relative motion between a wire loop and a magnetic field.
Faraday's law gives the direction of the induced emf.
Lenz's law gives the magnitude of the induced emf.
An induced emf is always in a direction that increases the change in the original magnetic flux that causes it.
The change in the magnetic flux is a decrease or an increase in the magnetic lines of force.
The change in the magnetic flux is a change in the magnetic lines of force.
When the north pole of a magnet is moved towards the coil, the magnetic flux through the coil, increases.
When a magnet is moved towards a coil, the induced current in the coil must produce an opposing magnetic field.
When the north pole of a magnet is moved towards the coil, the direction of the magnetic field produced by the induced current is towards the magnet.
When the north pole of a magnet is moved towards the coil, the end of the coil nearer the magnet must act as a north pole.
When the north pole of a magnet is moved towards the coil, the current in the coil must be flowing in an anticlockwise direction if viewed from the magnet.
When the north pole of a magnet is moved away from the coil, the end of the coil nearer the magnet must act as a south pole.
When the north pole of a magnet is moved away from the coil, the current in the coil flows in an anticlockwise direction if viewed from the magnet.
The induction coil is one of the useful applications of electromagnetic induction.
The apparatus that develops an electric spark is called an induction coil.
The induction coil was also known as the Ruhmkorff spark coil.
The induction coil consists of a soft iron core.
The primary coil of the induction coil uses a thin wire.
The secondary coil of the induction coil uses a thin wire.
In an induction coil, the primary has more turns than the secondary.
Electromagnetic waves are waves that do not require a medium to travel.
Light is an example of an electromagnetic wave.
Sound waves require a medium for propagation.
The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 3.0 x 10^8 m/s.
Electric field lines point away from negative charges.
The SI unit of electric charge is the Ampere.
Ohm's Law states that current is directly proportional to voltage.
The unit of resistance is the Ohm.
Power in an electrical circuit is measured in Watts.
A series circuit has only one path for current to flow.
In a parallel circuit, the voltage across each branch is the same.
Magnetic field lines form closed loops.
Like poles of magnets attract each other.
The Earth acts like a giant magnet.
A transformer can step up or step down AC voltage.
Transformers work on the principle of mutual induction.
Electrons have a positive charge.
A capacitor stores electrical energy in an electric field.
An inductor stores electrical energy in a magnetic field.
The frequency of a wave is the inverse of its period.
Wavelength is the distance between two consecutive crests of a wave.
Interference is a property of all types of waves.
Diffraction is the bending of waves around obstacles.
Visible light has a higher frequency than X-rays.
Ultraviolet light has a shorter wavelength than infrared light.
The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.
Refraction is the change in direction of a wave due to a change in speed.
Total internal reflection can occur when light travels from a denser to a rarer medium.
Convex lenses always form real images.
Concave mirrors can form virtual images.
An ammeter is connected in parallel to measure current.
A voltmeter is connected in parallel to measure potential difference.
Kirchhoff's current law is based on the conservation of charge.
Kirchhoff's voltage law is based on the conservation of energy.
A semiconductor has electrical conductivity between a conductor and an insulator.
Doping is the process of adding impurities to a semiconductor to change its properties.
A p-n junction allows current to flow easily in one direction but not the other.
Transistors can be used as switches or amplifiers.
An integrated circuit contains many electronic components on a single chip.
Radio waves have the longest wavelength in the electromagnetic spectrum.
Gamma rays have the highest energy in the electromagnetic spectrum.
The photoelectric effect demonstrates the particle nature of light.
De Broglie's hypothesis suggests that matter exhibits wave-like properties.
Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that position and momentum cannot be simultaneously measured with infinite precision.
The atomic nucleus contains protons and electrons.
Nuclear fission is the process of splitting a heavy nucleus into lighter nuclei.
Nuclear fusion is the process of combining light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus.
Alpha particles are essentially helium nuclei.
Beta particles are high-speed electrons or positrons.
Gamma rays are high-energy photons.
Half-life is the time required for half of a radioactive sample to decay.
The mass number of an atom is the sum of protons and neutrons.
Isotopes have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
Forces are vectors, meaning they have both magnitude and direction.
Newton's first law is also known as the law of inertia.
Newton's second law is F = ma.
Newton's third law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.
Work is done when a force causes an object to move.
Kinetic energy is the energy of motion.
Potential energy is stored energy based on an object's position.
Conservation of energy means energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed.
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity.
Impulse is equal to the change in momentum.
In an elastic collision, both kinetic energy and momentum are conserved.
Angular velocity is the rate of change of angular position.
Torque is the rotational equivalent of force.
The moment of inertia depends on the distribution of mass relative to the axis of rotation.
